items.headline: Eight in vivo CAR T biotechs to follow closely
items.preview: In vivo CAR-T therapies are gaining traction for their efficiency in treating cancers. This article highlights eight innovative companies in this space.
items.article:
In vivo CAR-T therapies have grown in demand as scientists tackle challenges of target specificity and safety, proving more efficient than ex vivo treatments for cancers and autoimmune conditions. Unlike conventional CAR-T therapy, which requires extensive manufacturing and patient chemotherapy, in vivo CAR-Ts deliver genetic instructions directly into the patient's bloodstream, significantly reducing wait times and costs.
Why this matters: While no in vivo CAR-Ts have been approved yet, the advancements in this field could revolutionize cell therapy, making it more accessible and effective for patients.
The article details eight companies, including Umoja Biopharma and Vyriad, that are pioneering in vivo CAR-T therapies, showcasing their lead candidates and recent developments.

items.headline: FDA opens path for uniQure’s Huntington’s gene therapy filing
items.preview: uniQure is set to file for accelerated approval of its Huntington's disease therapy AMT-130, following FDA's acceptance of Phase I/II data.
items.article:
uniQure is preparing to file its Huntington's disease candidate AMT-130 for accelerated approval in the US after the FDA indicated it would accept three-year Phase I/II data as the primary basis for the application. This marks a significant shift in the regulatory approach, allowing uniQure to submit its Biologics License Application in Q3 2026.
Why this matters: AMT-130 could become the first disease-modifying therapy for Huntington's disease, addressing a critical unmet need as current treatments do not slow disease progression. The FDA's willingness to consider smaller datasets may signal a more flexible approach for rare disease therapies moving forward.

items.headline: Can Diabetes Stem Cell Therapy in Colombia Offer US Patients Better Health?
items.preview: Colombia presents a cost-effective alternative for US patients seeking diabetes stem cell therapy, with significant savings and advanced treatments.
items.article:
Colombia has emerged as a viable option for US patients seeking diabetes stem cell therapy, offering high-dose Mesenchymal Stem Cell (MSC) treatments at a fraction of the cost. With comprehensive treatment packages, patients can save up to 70% compared to US prices.
Why this matters: The regulatory environment in Colombia allows for higher cell counts and advanced protocols, making it an attractive destination for those looking for effective diabetes management solutions. This guide outlines the benefits, treatment efficacy, and logistics for US patients considering this option.

items.headline: Can Brazilian Patients Find Pain Relief with Spine Arthritis Stem Cell Therapy in Mexico?
items.preview: Mexican clinics offer high-dose stem cell therapies for spine arthritis, providing a solution for Brazilian patients facing regulatory restrictions at home.
items.article:
With Brazil's ANVISA regulations limiting access to high-dose stem cell therapies, many Brazilian patients are turning to Mexico for treatment of spine arthritis. Mexican clinics can legally administer over 100 million MSCs, significantly enhancing therapeutic potential at a lower cost.
Why this matters: This shift highlights the growing trend of medical tourism as patients seek effective and affordable treatments abroad, particularly in countries with more flexible regulatory environments. The article discusses the advantages, costs, and success rates of stem cell therapy in Mexico for Brazilian patients.

items.headline: The microglia-derived protein sema4ab attenuates regenerative neurogenesis after spinal cord injury in zebrafish
items.preview: Research reveals that the protein sema4ab from microglia inhibits neurogenesis post-spinal cord injury in zebrafish, offering insights for regenerative medicine.
items.article:
A study on zebrafish demonstrates that the microglia-derived protein sema4ab plays a crucial role in inhibiting regenerative neurogenesis following spinal cord injury. Disruption of sema4ab leads to increased progenitor cell and neuron generation, although it also impairs axon regrowth and functional recovery.
Why this matters: Understanding the mechanisms by which sema4ab regulates neurogenesis could inform future strategies for enhancing spinal cord regeneration in non-regenerating vertebrates, potentially leading to novel therapeutic approaches for spinal injuries.
